name: Sonny Stitt
abstract: Sonny Stitt (born Edward Hammond Boatner Jr.; February 2, 1924 – July 22, 1982) was an American jazz saxophonist of the bebop/hard bop idiom. Known for his warm tone, he was one of the best-documented saxophonists of his era, recording over 100 albums.

about_quotes.speaker: Buster Williams
about_quotes.evidence: the first time I went out on the road was with Gene Ammons and Sonny Stitt... after the first set Gene Ammons and Sonny Stitt took me aside... asked me to stay with the band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Buster Williams interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 6, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: Sonny Stitt comes to town two or three years later and he takes me to see Sonny Stitt. And I got to meet him and I got to watch him

about_quotes.speaker: Sam Jones
about_quotes.evidence: This band with Sonny Stitt, Wynton Kelly and myself, Jimmy Cobb, Candido. This was an excellent band--small band.

about_quotes.speaker: JJ Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: Sonny was a dear friend – a real, real dear friend. We played a few times in some of the jazz venues in Chicago. We did travel together quite a few times.
about_quotes.doc_title: JJ Johnson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Percy Heath
about_quotes.evidence: We went on this thing with Katea’s father, Sonny [Stitt] was on it, and Dizzy, and John and I, and Mel Lewis and Hank Jones, it was a big all-star thing
about_quotes.doc_title: Percy Heath — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Easley
about_quotes.evidence: Sonny Stitt took me under his wings maybe about that time or whatever, and I used to hang with Sonny, and I’d try to pick Sonny’s brain.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bill Easley interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, November 1, 2021
